content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. The Ninth Gate (1999)

**Score:** 47
**Body:** That was a good watch! A oldschool mystery thriller similar to Da Vinci Code. One of these flicks from a time where movies were still GOOD, where you feel like watching an actual M.O.V.I.E.  At times a bit cheesy, but overall I enjoyed it a lot.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The cheesy is deliberate.


I think it's a rare, perfect movie.
2. I love the Ninth Gate. I put it on to go to sleep sometimes. Very interesting profession that guy has. “Book detective”. He makes it look like a bad boy thing haha.
3. If you can find a copy of the book it's based on - it is worth a read.  😄 
  - The Club Dumas
    - Alexandree Dumbass?!
4. I love the bit when the girl's eyes change from human to devil. Cold, emotionless eyes. It's amazing.
5. Yeah, cheesy as hell but it's kinda fun. My favorite part is when Depp bludgeons the guy with his shoe.
6. That scene with the woman in the wheelchair still gives me nightmares!!!   
7. This is one of my favourite films. It has that Hammer Horror vibe about it 
8. I compulsively re-watch this every few months.

## 2. What's a good movie to watch after experiencing a huge setback in your life?

**Score:** 36
**Body:** I just got fired from my job at a public library. I was at the end of a six-month probationary period after getting promoted from part-time to full-time, and they told me my performance was "unacceptable." Even if I wasn't a model employee, I still tried my best. My supervisor reprimanded me a few times, but I always listened to their criticism and worked hard to correct my behavior. 

This is so devastating because I liked that job. I was unemployed for a whole year before the library hired me. It's like life gave me a second chance and I blew it. 

Can someone suggest me a movie to give me a little hope? Something about a person starting again after hitting rock bottom? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watch Cast Away. Tom Hanks on a desert island. 

Think about how you are not on a desert island. 
2. Harold and Maude
3. The Truman Show.

Walk out that door. There's something wonderful on the other side. 
4. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
5. It’s A Wonderful Life. 
6. Chef. 
7. Brittany Runs a Marathon
8. Life is long. This is just a stumble on a long road. You can exclude this job on your resume since it was only six months. You’ll find something else. I would suggest Life Of Pi because it’s about overcoming extraordinary struggles. Good luck.👍🏼 
9. Send Help on Hulu. Rachel McAdams is a badass
10. The Intern with Robert De Niro and Anne Hathaway always puts a smile on my face :)

## 3. Movies where you actually ROOT/ support the bad guy/ criminals?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** “Falling Down” (1993) starring Michael Douglas, “One Hour Photo” (2002) starring the late Robin Williams both come into my mind (and maybe some crime themed comedies like 1999’s, “Happy, Texas” with Steve Zahn) also comes into my mind as well, but anything else of the criteria’s/ themes at all/ related to?

Thanks as always fellow cinema fanatics! 👍

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Raising Arizona
  - I think I got the best one! 
2. Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid
3. Law Abiding Citizen
4. Goodfellas. 
5. Inside Man (2006)
6. Ocean’s 11 
7. Airheads (1994) - starring Brendan Fraser, Steve Buscemi and Adam Sandler
8. Payback 
9. The Devil's Rejects. It's hard to believe but by the end of the movie you are rooting for the redneck torture family.

## 4. Jaw droppingly disgusting comedy movies like John Waters

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Looking for films that have the same rebellious, disgusting, comedic shock value that you can find all throughout Waters career. Scenes that are so wild, you're just stunned at the audacity of the director. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Before he made became famous and made blockbusters like *Lord of the Rings*, Peter Jackson began his career with three disgusting, micro-budget indie classics:

*Bad Taste* (1987)

*Meet the Feebles* (1989)

*Braindead* (1992)

All are great and messed up in their own ways. 

Edit: All are horror comedies by the way.
2. The Greasy Strangler 

i dont recommend it for everyone unless they are a Hootie Tootie Disco Cutie.
  - Am indeed a bullshit artist
3. The Greasy Strangler

Technically a “horror”,  but kinda funny in the same way as a John Waters movie.

4. The Toxic Avenger

Faster Pussycat Kill Kill
5. Are you familiar with the director Yorgos Lanthimos? I recommend starting with Dogtooth, or Kinds of Kindness
6. Todd Solondz films
7. Pretty much any Russ Meyer movie
8. Try getting into Lloyd Kaufman next. The Toxic Avenger series (particularly the fourth one), Terror Firmer, Poultrygeist, Tromeo and Juliet, etc might be up your alley.
9. The Cook, the Thief, his Wife, and her Lover

## 5. Wacky dark comedies about a crime that spirals out of control that AREN’T Coen Bros or Guy Richie

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Something like fargo, burn after reading, snatch, lock stock and two smoking barrels etc but NOT by those directors I have seen everything they have made. 

It was just an accident (2025), killer joe (2011) and good time. fit this well I think too. Someone commits a crime they didnt fully think through and things spiral out of control. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sam Raimi is Coen brothers adjacent, but: *A Simple Plan*
  - Think i am going to watch this one tonight. Ive heard good things about it before and love billy bob. Thanks!
2. Very Bad Things
3. Seven Psycopaths
4. Kiss Kiss, Bang Bang, The Nice Guys. Shane Black writes a lot in this genre 
5. Surprised that A Fish Called Wanda hasn't made an appearance yet. It's one of the OG versions of this trope done for fun. 
6. Heathers!!
7. In Bruges fits this description pretty well!  

Wacky and unpredictable: check

Crime spiraling out of control:  check

Dark comedy: alllllll the checks.

More bleak than the Coen Brothers though.
8. Big Nothing (2006)
9. Family Plot, Alfred Hitchcock's final film. 

Shallow Grave, with a young Ewan McGregor

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Stellan Skarsgård was terrific in The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo. A perfect crime thriller with sharp cinematography. What’s your opinion on this Fincher flick, and where would you rank it in his filmography?

**Score:** 680

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love so much that Orinoco Flow by Enya is playing during this torture scene it’s so perfect.  
  - Apparently that was Daniel Craig's idea too
2. [deleted]
3. Still pisses me off that we never got the sequels even though Fincher, Mara, and Craig all wanted to do them 
4. Swedish original I found actually better. Even if Fincher is top notch.
  - Never watched this versión, the original had me at the edge of the seat.
  - The girl who kicked the hornet’s nest was my favorite of the 3 but I agree that the original Swedish version was better than its American counterpart. It was good but didn’t quite live up to the original.
5. That exchange always reminds me of a motto on a crime podcast I used to listen to, My Favorite Murder. They always said “Fuck politeness”, because of all the people who had been killed because they were polite to someone and got exploited for it. And I agree. If something feels wrong, go with your gut. 

Someone walking towards you down the street gives you a weird vibe? Cross the street.

Feel like someone is following you? Run away.

Random person asks for help doing something? Recruit more help, don’t go alone.

Sure these people if innocent may find you rude or weird, but if they have ulterior motives, you could spoil their plans and keep on living.

So yeah, “fuck politeness”.
6. I like the Swedish version better like most folks, but good lord Trent Reznor went apeshit with the Immigrant Song 🤯
7. Masterpiece. Rooney was perfect. 

## 2. "I'll Take 3 Weeks at #1 Over Good Reviews Any Day": The War of the Worlds Producer Has Zero Regrets After 5 Razzies

**Score:** 425

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That's all you need to know. He's not in it for the art or act of creating, he's in it for the money.
  - Isn’t that the whole point of Hollywood?
    - If there's anything I learned bitch this game is about MOTHERFUCKING MONEY
      - Kino reference 
      - I got that dollar yall
2. “I’ll take people clicking on a thing and hating it than actually enjoying and engaging with it any day”
  - People engage with content they hate more than things they love, I'd reckon
3. The Room of our generation 
  - But 'The Room' only came out in...

I mean it's not like it's...

I'm still...

...

Oh.
4. 3 weeks at #1 on amazon is not 3 weeks at #1.

## 3. ↻Antagonists that aren't evil or a jerk, but just the opposition of the protagonist

**Score:** 389

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|L4FEYn7StSqNq)
2. ![gif](giphy|lyqfLDqMTFhU4)
  - Perfect. I love Sam and his whole team, but I still want Richard to find the real killer. He is the antagonist the whole way through, but he's just doing his job.
3. Colin Farrell as Danny Witwer in Minority Report. 

https://preview.redd.it/xrjhjs9a020h1.jpeg?width=600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=aca534d11ebfcd675ab982c8d0e4d56b8ab57e4f

He is initially introduced as an antagonist to Tom Cruise, and appears to be an arrogant dick sent to undermine the Precrime program. As the movie goes on, it becomes clearer and clearer that the character is integral in holding the Justice system accountable for its faults.
  - I like him. As soon as he realized that Anderton was innocent, he immediately stops chasing him and starts investigating the true criminal. 
4. https://preview.redd.it/h1ajfhgyy10h1.jpeg?width=305&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=749b5fdfc6cc7327baf91a73daed37b8fece7731

Judge Chamberlain Haller from My Cousin Vinny. 
5. Hear me out, not the actor, but Principal Ed Rooney had a shitbird student skipping class and causing other students to skip class, break the rules, donate to fake charities, and basically scam everybody. 

If he does nothing the rules don't matter, so hell yes, he goes after him when he kidnaps two students and crashes a ferrari. 
  - It's universally accepted that Ferris is just a dick. There's been a decade's worth of YouTube videos talking about it. 
6. Carl Hanratty, Catch Me if You Can 
7. One of my favorite lines from this movie is “YOU’RE A LOUSY FUCKING SOFTBALL PLAYER, JACK!”

## 4. What’s your favorite Zombie movie?

**Score:** 355
**Body:** Amongst a plethora of zombie flicks, what is that one movie that stands out you?

For me it’s 28 Days Later. It’s a perfect movie to me.

Cillian Murphy is fantastic, and the score by John Murphy is perfection. The humans being more monstrous in the end than the zombies themselves was horrifying. 

What’s your favorite zombie movie?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://i.redd.it/fh1j2bvnu60h1.gif

Dawn of the Dead
  - That opening scene is 🔥
2. The intro to Dawn of the Dead 2004 is still one of the best “initial outbreak” scenes and solidified the “running zombies” alongside 28 days later 
  - That opening is incredible.

Snyder in the 2000’s was on fire. Dawn of the dead, 300, watchmen, etc. 
3. does rec count? probably that or 28 days later 
4. Shaun of the Dead (2004).
  - ![gif](giphy|p0uwbQB2fBSP6)
5. Or Shaun of the Dead!

https://preview.redd.it/ll3461s2u60h1.jpeg?width=1045&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6805ee8b6e2906cfbf861cebcd31549632c746d6
6. Return of the Living Dead (1985)
7. REC (2007)

## 5. What is the Best Performance of Academy Award Winner and 7x Nominee Emma Stone?

**Score:** 271

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Easy A
  - YES. Also Tucci is amazing
    - “I’m adopted.” “What?! Oh my god! Who told you?!”
    - “It sounds like you're having sex in here...Which can't be true due to the fact that you have a homosexual boyfriend”
  - ![gif](giphy|t2L7vQqVGcMYo)
2. Superbad
3. I didn't realize she's been nominated seven times!
  - 3 times in lead actress (La La Land, Poor Things, and Bugonia) 2 times in supporting actress (Birdman & The Favourite) 2 times in best picture as a producer (Poor Things & Bugonia)
4. *Maniac* and *The Curse*.
5. Easy A idgaf 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Why the Coen brothers set The Big Lebowski in the recent past

**Score:** 175
**Body:** The film was released in 1998, but it is set a few years earlier during the Gulf War, maybe sometime in 90/91. Apart from a few funny references to Bush and Iraq (this aggression will not stand), it isn't about this specific time period and this setting doesn't have any bearing on the events of the plot. You could conceivably have set the film in 1998 and on paper very little would change, even the phone/beeper wouldn't have been too out of date. But obviously it is a very conscious choice for the film to be *kind-of* a period piece. 

So why did the Coens choose that setting? Now it could be something simplistic like "they just wrote the story back then" but my feeling is that they had a couple of intentions:

A lot of the characters are kind of like people unstuck in time who don't really belong in the modern era, or even in this film. Dude is a burned out hippie, Walter can't move on from Vietnam (or a lot of things really... literally says "you're goddamn right I'm livin in the past" when they're talking about his ex wife), even the narrator is a cowboy sitting in a neon bowling alley, like they're in some kind of purgatory for old movie characters. The Dude doesn't even belong *in this story*, he's a stoner dragged into a Raymond Chandler noir by mistaken identity. 

Much of the movie seems to be about the kind of incomprehensible history and identity of America - especially California/Hollywood - how it keeps changing, and how hard it is to really get a handle on all of it, and half the movie is spent with characters introducing themselves, mistaking someone for someone else, or questioning who someone is and what they're doing in this story (some variation of "Who the fuck is X?" must be repeated a dozen times). 

And as much as the movie is a farce, it's also kind of melancholy and elegiac in the end. By putting the film just a few years in the past it's still "contemporary" and recognisable, but this version of America and these characters have already slipped away before the story has even began. And ending on the note of "the Dude abides" it's a kind of acceptance of the passage of time - it's a bewildering, confusing world, things keep changing and it's impossible to really comprehend it, just try not to take it too hard. 

Just my interpretation, what do others think?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think there are a few ideas at work, and certainly this sense of the story being “not of our time” is one of them. 

I also think there are some practical considerations. Between 1990 and 1998 a few things happened. Cell phone use exploded, as did personal computers and email. The internet came into existence. Competitive bowling also took a nose dive. 

The Big Lebowski is a very “analog” story. Putting it in a digital world would fundamentally alter its narrative. 

  - Also it just takes years to make movies. They may have written it in the time it was in and by the time it actually got produced and released it was then in the “recent past”. 
    - You are correct.

They actually wrote the screenplay around the same time as Barton Fink, which came out in 1991. 

Which is also wild because they said that the relationship between Fink and Mundt informed the relationship between The Dude and Walter. 
  - The one crucial digital element is that toe
2. I like this write-up a lot, I rewatched it recently and had some similar thoughts but didn’t flesh them out this much. 

I think a lot of the characters kinda represent archetypes of the 70s who all find themselves leading lives that might be considered a disappointment to their younger selves, but which they’re mostly able to find some kind of satisfaction in. The Dude was a real left-wing organizer, Walter was a proud soldier, even characters like Jackie Treehorn seem to have found themselves a bit adrift in what was then the modern era. Nobody is miserable with their lot in life, they just all ended up not living in the world they thought they would when they were younger. Even the Big Lebowski, whose ideal world is closest to the one we were living in by the early 90s, isn’t truly happy with how things turned out - hell, he’s probably the most miserable character in the whole film. He lives a life without any real human connection and has a huge chip on his shoulder about “bums” and whether he commands respect or not. Because he responds to this insecurity by putting up a false front, marrying a trophy wife who doesn’t care about him, and pretending to be more respectable than he is, he’s unhappy on a much deeper level than The Dude, or Walter, or Maude. Everyone else accepted that the world they live in wasn’t the ideal one they wanted and made something of their lives anyway, but The Big Lebowski, faced with a world he supposedly wanted, isn’t able to come to terms with it. 
3. I've also thought about this a lot, and I agree. That said, I do think the little details that set this movie in that period have aged it very well.

Sometimes, when a movie is set during the year it comes out, we don't have as firm an understanding of the era as we will a few years later, which can date the film. Think of any movie using a pop culture reference that quickly becomes irrelevant or controversial. Setting your film in even the recent past allows you to curate everything more carefully, in a way that ages better.

Honestly, just starting the movie with Sam Elliott's "this took place in the early 90s" narration does a lot to keep it feeling self aware, like it's in on the joke of the era WITH the audience, instead of being the subject of laughter itself.

Regardless of all that, I do agree with your thesis. The film is a bizarre hodgepodge of forgotten people in a constantly shifting American colaipdascope. That's why the older you get, the more relatable The Dude becomes.
4. It seems like it’s part of their process. Most (not all) of their movies are very specific about time and place, and they load in a few references to the political backdrop. I suspect the national politics are also a jumping off point for some of the themes they put into the movie.

Raising Arizona has a similar vibe, as it has some conspicuous references to the ‘84 presidential election, though it was made in ‘86. As much as it is a mashup of various older movies and books, it also seemed to be a response to the ‘84 presidential election too.

5. When Robert Altman made The Long Goodbye, he was adapting a detective noir written in the early 50’s, but he made it contemporary; by the early 70’s, being a private eye was an increasingly comical and ridiculous thing to be. In my opinion, The Big Lebowski is just pushing this even further—he wasn’t even a proper private eye to begin with, it wouldn’t make any sense.
6. having no personal memory of what distinguishes 1998 and 1991 i cant really help elucidate what they're searching for, but there's a parallel 'period piece by a few years' in Todd Haynes' Safe (released 1995, set in 1987) partially due to cultural reference (the 'AIDS as moral disease' works differently in 1987 than 1995 although its not explicitly named) but also because of as you say a certain quality of desired elegy. both films arrive in the Clinton era, but are interested in the Reagan/Bush national culture. Haynes is doing more explicit cultural commentary, but Lebowski as you point out, is also keenly interested and aware of the changing identities of America and California. these are people that America is reforming away from, a certain Easy Rider attitude, although perhaps interestingly Easy Rider doesn't have this same phenomenon: it's from 1969 and also set in 1969, even as it acknowledges that the 1969 its depicting is dead.
7. It’s not as complex as you might think:

*The Big Lebowski* is set during the Gulf War of the early 1990s, but the Coen brothers weren't out to make a strong political statement. Rather, they needed something that Walter — The Dude's right-wing, tough-talking sidekick — could be upset about. 

"Setting the film during the Gulf War was an opportunity to have Walter gas about something," said Ethan in an [interview](http://www.indiewire.com/1998/03/the-coens-speak-reluctantly-83037/). "And it's more attractive to make something time specific than just present day." In one of the film's dream sequences, former Iraqi president Saddam Hussein is even seen working at a bowling alley and handing The Dude a pair of bowling shoes.

\- taken from a CBC article. 

## 2. Aftersun (2022) absolutely broke me

**Score:** 36
**Body:** Wonderfully executed. You don't see that movie, you feel it. 

The way the videotape footage is captured.

The late 90s / early 2000s feel.

Pure nostalgia.

No other movie made me feel this way and I was delaying watching it in case of a future 4k release (does not need it at all).

>!Nothing about the story is complicated, half through and towards the end you start connecting the dots on the alternate timelines/dancefloor.!<  
>!Having dealt with depression and knowing how it is feeling you don't deserve happiness is portrayed in a way I've never seen before.!<

  
There's no point in the movie I found it "pretentious" of something that it wasn't and the actors chemistry was like watching indeed a father daughter relationship.  
  
I find that nostalgia here plays a huge role of how things are perceived.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It is one of my favorite movie of all time. The way it deals with depression is so realistic and grounded that sometimes I felt that I'm looking at my own life in this movie. And the performance of the father is so nuanced and great that it's like you can see the struggle in his eyes even when he's not talking much and the ending truly broke me.
  - Paul Mescal (Callum) was indeed great. 
You can catch a glimpse on what’s going on with him during that bus scene, the boats scene, the yoga on the balcony’s rails scene, the mirror scene inside the room where Sophie was feeling down. I totally knew what was going on and at the same time did not want to believe it.
    - For me it was just one line when she asks him "wish we could just stay for longer" and you can see in his eyes just for a split second that he knew it's the last time they would be together as he just smiled and nod
2. I just watched this in the past week and haven’t been able to stop thinking about it. I have trouble remembering the last time a film affected me in such a visceral way. As someone who’s struggled with depression since childhood, it hit so close to home. 
  - Yes that's what stuck with me. 

I've seen lots of technically better, better written movies, but the feeling this one gave me was unreal.

I usually like Lynchesque movies where not many things make sense and you give them meaning, these usually make me "feel" more, rather than understanding something.

Here everything makes sense and it totally hit like a truck.

I'm not even a father and I felt like I lived a part of it.
3. I’m not sure I understand why it’s being framed as a depression film here? I don’t think it’s very important to diagnose the dad? Like I guess I’m not understanding how that label changes anything; I understand that having depression makes his character more relatable but why can’t he just be confronting a loss he’s had of his wife and the coming loss of his daughter.

The language of pathology is sort of irrelevant or a non-sequitur here because there is perfect situational reason to be sad.

## 3. The Devil Wears Prada | I liked it even though I didn’t quite get it

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Great storytelling for a story I don’t really care about. But the way it’s told is so good that it almost made me care. I really liked that idea that if you join a team of people who are passionate about something then you should either match their passion or leave. Setting aside the realities of the job market, of course.

That theme is undermined by two things: Miranda and Nate.

Miranda is a singular force that drives her magazine, she’s great at her job. She also seems to take a lot of pleasure in lording over her underlings. She’s passionate about her job but is clearly a narcissistic sadist who enjoys the feeling of power. Andy eventually rejects her and the path she represents in her life. The problem is because Miranda is almost a cult leader it feels less like Andy learning to appreciate the passion her colleagues have for something she considers beneath her and more like Andy being sucked into Miranda’s cult of personality.

But even if we take that as the writing being nuanced and complex— Miranda is both a narcissist who abuses power but is also a passionate mentor with something to teach about work ethic— there’s still Nate. 

Nate never appreciates Andy’s growing passion for the job (if we take it as passion and not falling into Miranda’s cult). In the end Andy comes around to see things his way to the point of apologising to him. Andy is not a perfect protagonist (she’s a great character) and she might have things to apologise for but if Nate is right about Andy changing then I’m not sure what the film is saying ultimately or what Andy’s journey is about.

She starts out as her own woman and ends as her own woman. Maybe she learns to dress better but then she’s beautifully dressed even before Nigel’s lessons like in the dinner scene with Nate and their friends. At first I thought the film was commenting on the fashion industry by showing her as someone who can dress well outside of Runway’s objective standards but then the film tries to argue for fashion as art (which was a pleasant surprise). Then it ultimately rejects the whole thing. 

If it was just a rejection of Miranda’s way of running things it would’ve been clearer but Andy rejects the whole thing and goes to do “real” journalism. Then it turns out Miranda has a heart of gold that can nurture and recognise talent. She doesn’t get Andy blocklisted for quitting in the middle of an important job. 

Or maybe I’m missing something.

Anyway I really enjoyed the movie despite my confusion about its themes. The cast is excellent. Anne Hathaway and Meryl Streep are the attention grabbing performances (in a good way) but Stanley Tucci and Emily Blunt did so much with what could’ve been one note characters. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's a character portrait of the cost of power and ambition. The movie does not over-assert that Miranda is wrong. If anything, its point is that she's right within this world. The wrong comes from the question of whether that cost is worthwhile in light of what it does to her relationships.

The Devil Wears Prada is a tonally more accessible version of Whiplash, admittedly a movie that came later. Whiplash is also a little more overtly clear that all of this is wrong.

In both cases, though, lots of people will say, "Hey, you wanna run with the big dogs, this is what it looks like."

The thing is, both movies take their villains seriously and present their villains' view of whether the cost is worth it seriously. The price of that story structure is that the villain is hard to fully reject.
  - I also just watched this film and one of the standout lines to me was from Nigel when Andi is venting about her personal life being on a thin wire, a confession met with somewhat resigned law-of-the-jungle  observation "Let me know when your whole life goes up in smoke. Means it's time for a promotion."

I think the juxtaposition of personal sacrifice(and not really in a romantic framing) and professional grit(in the big dog world at least) is pretty consistently present throughout the whole film, across all the characters, in many varieties so your reading really holds as a thematic throughline to me.

I can't help but also think of Sinners which I rewatched recently, observing that the Vampire's proposition seemed somewhat difficult to rebuke "objectively". Rather embracing the Vamp commune in that film results in pretty clear benefits but at the cost of some ephemeral quality of being that gets lost in the assimilation. 

In the case of both, it might've been easier to paint the side of the rejected choice as strictly a pure negative and straightforward decision - yet while both have a clear sympathetic and antagonistic side, the seriousness of the offered "deal with the devil" gives dimension to what could be a very cheap conflict otherwise.


As an aside, one interesting thing I noticed was that Nate works in a NYC kitchen, clearly with ambition as he becomes a Sous Chef, a famously high stress, toxic, and consuming work environment. Yet despite what I would imagine is a very similar parallel to the highly stressful and toxic fashion world, it seems to me that he is the one character where the theme doesn't really show up(except to demonstrate the cost of *Andi's* situation). I'm not saying the movie would be better or anything, I think the friends/BF are the least interesting part of the movie by far but I couldn't help but take note of the choice to have him working in a space that so parallels the Fashion world(on a certain level of abstraction anyways) and never really do anything with it
    - More than anything, the big takeaway is "Please, take your villains serious!"

Yes, the boyfriend in The Devil Wears Prada is a thin accessory of a character that you don't want to think too hard about (there are a million movies with this thin love interest problem).

The boyfriend is an entire college class discussion waiting to happen about writing problems and the thin solutions you get just because that's what fits into a two-hour movie. For example, if his life was also hard, then he wouldn't be a respite from her life. Never mind that the people he works with have knives and probably a few drug problems. *(I read Kitchen Confidential; I know things!)*

There is an alternate universe where The Devil Wears Prada is a five-season show on FX, and the season-three episode that follows the boyfriend through a double at the restaurant is a top-ten episode of all television for the 2010s.

The difference is just what you get when you have to compress characters for a two-hour movie.
2. I think my interpretation of the film is about how different characters try to sculpt this young woman. The Andy that Nate wants and the Andy that Nigel and Miranda want are totally different people and Andy is forced to chose what she values more. She "screws over" Emily to get to Paris before realizing that to advance in her job she'll need to take that uncaring attitude to everyone she works with. Nigel expects her to become more committed to fashion and Runway (fair) and also expects her to lose weight and happily undergo workplace abuse (unfair!). Nate's expectation of her is also toxic since he has no room for her to change and grow, and that relationship would always have imploded even without her taking on a high-stress job. Miranda and Nigel force her to learn how to be a better worker, but she is unwilling to become a narcissistic boss or someone who happily bears their boss's abuse. Nate reminds her of her own value while also holding her back from truly realizing it. It's a coming of age movie, Andy has to decide who she'll be at the end, and I think after the movie she'll take something from all three
3. The Devil Wears Prada is a masterpiece of its genre. As you observed, the storytelling is wonderfully efficient and solid, and the cast is superb, featuring an iconic performance from an iconic actress (Streep, obviously). It's just a great movie. 

To me it makes most sense when viewed as a Cinderella story. The characters fit pretty neatly into the Cinderella universe - Cinderella (Andy), Stepmother (Miranda), Stepsister (Emily), Mother (Nigel), Prince (Christian). The Runway office is the family's house and Paris fashion week is the ball. 

The film chooses to complicate Cinderella's journey by adding Nate and her friend group. As far as I know there are no 'villagers' in the original Cinderella myth, but if there were these new characters would be them - a boy Cinderella has a romantic attachment with and a bunch of guys they meet down the tavern. They serve to provide Cinderella with a level of authenticity - let's face it, in the original story she's fairly superficial. 

The twist on the story is that when this Cinderella runs from the ball (leaving her shoe/pager behind her), she does so voluntarily, not in fear, but with a new found sense of agency and power. She escapes the tyranny of her Stepmother's house not by marrying the Prince, but by returning to live independently among the villagers, taking up once more with her old boyfriend there. It's a cool twist appropriate to the times. 

For deeper analysis of themes we can then turn to the vast literature that exists concerning the Cinderella myth. Be warned - there's a lot of it. The power of this story (as with all foundational myths) is in its capacity to be endlessly analysed, deconstructed and reinterpreted.
4. I just watched it for the first time the other day, and what stood out to me was that Andy observed that no one would criticize Miranda if she were a man, that that kind of ambition and drive would be applauded, which is true to a large extent, but she doesn't seem to apply that thinking to herself. She lets her friends and her boyfriend make her feel guilty and bad about herself for being absolutely dedicated to her work.

I mean ultimately she decides that that's not what she wants for herself, but I wish that she had been a little more self-aware on that point. Whether someone sacrifices their personal life to dedicate themselves to their career is a choice, and it's not automatically wrong just because it's not what other people want from them.
  - I didn’t really get the sense that her friends were unsupportive of her though.

They clearly let her do her thing for quite a while, and even her boyfriend supported her in this even though it is wildly different from her original passions which she had always talked about.

They only start to sour on her when she becomes a distant friend who misses important events, is barely even present when she is there, and who seemed to be openly flirting with other men.

The point of the film is that the job completely changes her without her even realizing it. Anyone who dances with the devil becomes enamored with the life, even if it’s one they never wanted in the first place.
5. Honestly I think it’s a bit of an incoherent movie that can’t decide which side it’s on. Originally the intention was simply that Miranda and her world are impressive but ultimately shallow and bad and although Andi figures out how excel there, she ultimately chooses something more true to herself. But Meryl’s performance was so iconic, and the dressing up so appealing, it became hard to stick to that. It’s hard not to admire work that is so demanding but also high quality that it requires your full attention. Personally I watch it now and I am grateful for the demanding mentors I had and wish i could rule with Miranda’s ironclad commitment to excellence. “Why is no one reaaaady” is in my head as I slog through another tragic Zoom meeting. 

## 4. Part 1: Watching 100 new movies in 1 year: 38/100 so far, ranked and reviewed in 1-2 sentences

**Score:** 7
**Body:** (I decided to break this post into 2 due to length. A Part 2 with my 5s, 4s, and 2s is coming.)

Five months into the year, I'm ready to catch up on posting about this. Before we start, here's a description of my rating system, where each film can earn out of 7 Singletons (based on the director John Singleton who you should know directed and wrote *Boyz n the Hood* (1991) and was nominated for Best Director as both the youngest and first Black director at only 24.)

7/7 Singletons: A movie that changed me, changed cinema, or is just great at what it does.

6/7 Singletons: A memorably good movie for me.

5/7 Singletons: A sufficiently pleasant movie, but not one I feel I'll remember. 

4/7 Singletons: This is a special category I'm going to call “He a Little Confused, But He Got the Spirit.” Reserved for movies like *Waterworld* (1995) and *The Baltimorons* (2025), these are movies where I love some parts and have problems with others.

3/7 Singletons: Another special category for movies that might be perfectly good, but that I find too disturbing to watch.

2/7 Singletons: A movie that had issues with narrative structure, characters, tension, etc. to the point that I kinda wish I had my time back and I feel Charlie Brown sad Christmas music about that.

1/7 Singletons: A movie that had issues to the point that I’m angry about it. I believe it’s nice not to yuck other people’s yums, but I'm gonna do it when it comes to these films.

As I'm looking at my list, I'm also realizing that ranking these films seems like and apples-and-oranges endeavor in some sense, and there's not a whole lot of distinction I feel between some of the 5s and 6s. Here's my current best attempt.

\--------------------------------

**1) 7/7 S:** ***Apocalypse Now*** **(1979):** I have no hesitation, however, in naming this the best movie I've seen so far this year. Just... how this became a movie still awes me. I place it next to *Space Odyssey,* just a tremendous, transformative journey of a movie. You watch this movie and *you got fucking movied.* 

**2) 7/7 S:** ***Boyz n the Hood*** **(1991):** I'd give 7/7 to just the first part of this film alone when the main characters are children, which almost watches like a short film. Funny, moving, perception-altering, top-tier cinema. 

**3) 7/7 S:** ***The Godfather Part II*** **(1974):** I really enjoyed spending time with baby Vito in old New York, and the scene where Fredo is lying so limp in that chair steals the whole movie.

**4) 7/7 S:** ***The Godfather*** **(1972):** One of the most well-constructed-"feeling" movies I've ever watched. It just hums.

**5) 7/7 S:** ***Slap Shot*** **(1977):** How the hell does this score so high with me, do you ask? Almost every line in this movie is so outrageously crass and quotable, and the film achieves something sublime in its cultural status among hockey players. 

**6) 7/7 S:** ***Godzilla Minus One*** **(2023):** This was my first Godzilla movie and wasn't prepared to be a crying mess or so moved by its bleak yet hopeful themes. 

**7) 7/7 S:** ***The Conversation*** **(1974):** Perhaps my favorite sound design in a movie ever.

**8) 7/7 S:** ***Sinners*** **(2025):** I'm 7ing this movie specifically for the scene where dancers and singers from all times populate the dancefloor. Just an utterly arresting tribute to the power of expression over time. 

**9) 7/7 S:** ***Misery*** **(1980):** 7ing for Kathy Bates' iconic performance.  

**10) 6/7 S:** ***Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore*** **(1974):** I was touched by this film's meditation on womanhood "off the rails"--what happens when you're divorced, or unmarried, or your life just doesn't look like what women's lives are "supposed" to.

**11) 6/7 S:** ***The Freshman*** **(1990):** Required viewing if you've seen the *Godfather* films. The script is so zany but takes itself seriously enough to pull off a tremendous amount of heart. 

**12) 6/7 S:** ***American Graffiti*** **(1973):** What I feel like Coppola's *The Outsiders* was trying to be. I love the music in this film--how the soundtrack is always playing on a radio somewhere.

**13) 6/7 S:** ***Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse*** **(1991):** Also required viewing if you've seen A*pocalypse Now.* Also, somehow just a great meditation on what it takes to live a life committed to your values and vision--and how finding yourself requires getting lost.

**14) 6/7 S:** ***When Harry Met Sally*** **(1989):** I'll name that I'm more 4/7 on the ending, but it's such a tremendously charming film with such true-to-life portrayals of relationships that I give it a pass.

**15) 6/7 S:** ***Barbershop*** **(2002):** Just a lovely movie about the importance of Black community. Especially meaningful to me as a Chicagoan--set in winter, the sound of cars driving through slush and the El train is immediately recognizable and gave me warm fuzzies. 

**16) 6/7 S:** ***The Long Walk*** **(2025):** I love this book and was blown away by how fucking bleak this is in live action. Superb acting by so many young actors who I hope to see more of.

**17) 6/7 S:** ***Frankenstein*** **(2025):** I feel that del Toro's aesthetic can get a little precious and impede his stories sometimes--but that did not happen here, maybe because it wasn't his story lol. I was very moved by Elordi's portrayal of The Creature.

**18) 6/7 S:** ***Tootsie*** **(1982):** What could've been a basic gender-bending comedy digs unexpectedly deep into women's issues and perspectives. Lange is wonderful and deserves her oscar. 

**19) 6/7 S:** ***Godzilla*** **(1954):** Since I watched *Minus One* I felt like I needed to see the original--and was likewise impressed with the pull-no-punches, bleak message about the horrors of nuclear war. 

**20) 6/7 S:** ***Hamnet*** **(2025):** I love that this movie is about famous, historic people experiencing a common, harrowing grief--and finding their way back to each other.

**21) 6/7 S:** ***Train Dreams*** **(2025):** I love quiet, meditative movies that don't feel like they're about much, but seem to stick with you. I'll remember images from this movie for a long time. Another fine meditation on grief, if you're into that.

**22) 6/7 S: Oscars Live Shorts (2025):** I know these aren't "a film" but I treated them as such. I thought *The Singers* and *Two People Exchanging Saliva* were better than a number of feature-length Oscars noms I saw this year.

**23) 6/7 S:** ***The Long Goodbye*** **(1973):** Memorable to me solely for the fact that Elliott Gould as Philip Marlowe is THE living embodiment of Spike Spiegel. I was entranced the entire film! I grieve that we can't have a live action Cowboy Bebop with him in it...

## 5. From Conrad Veidt to Christian Bale: the tragic “monster” on film

**Score:** 1
**Body:** ***The Man Who Laughs*** (1928) is said to be the inspiration for Batman’s joker. But I also find some similarities between Conrad Veidt portrayal of Gwynplaine and Christian Bale’s portrayal of  Frank in ***The Bride!*** They both appear monstrous but they are the result of a cruel society, and man’s need to control nature. The way both characters have to hide their face/identity from the world(**same scarf/handkerchief in both films**) to avoid being seen as a threat. It’s like they both play these chapters as a living wound:     https://imgur.com/a/vTm33tI every gesture and look carries this crushing loneliness, this sense of being deformed by how society sees them. 

Christian Bale’s monster has the Karloff “misunderstood outcast” vibe, but the loneliness and expressive physicality really reminded me of Conrad Veidt. Like the old German Expressionist idea where the distorted body is just a mirror of a distorted world kind of thing. 

Since ***The Bride!*** is set in 1930s Chicago maybe Gyllenhaal was inspired by the German expressionist film. 

Does this Expressionist reading of Bale’s performance make sense to you, or do you see it as grounded in a different acting tradition?
